1:
On 2 Corinthians 5:1 — “For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens” — the witnesses mark the contrast of the tent and the eternal house.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Robertson: “dissolved” (katalythe), the very word for striking down a tent; the “earthly house of our tabernacle” (1 Corinthians 15:40). Wesley: “only a tabernacle, or tent, not designed for a lasting habitation.” The Latin father Tertullian, against Marcion, insists the “eternal home in heaven” does not mean the body perishes forever. The Reformed Calvin reads it as an enriching of the foregoing comfort.

The verse sets the frail body against the eternal dwelling. If our earthly tent is taken down in death, we have a building from God, not made with hands, eternal in the heavens. The believer’s sure hope answers the body’s decay.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his courage under affliction in the certainty of the eternal house. The witnesses note the deliberate figure: the present body is a “tabernacle,” a tent — flimsy, temporary, soon struck down; but awaiting the believer is “a building of God,” permanent, of divine making, eternal in the heavens. They mark the confident “we know” — not a wish but an assured knowledge, the ground of Paul’s calm amid sufferings. They observe that the glorified, resurrection body (or the heavenly state) is set in sharp contrast to the dissolving tent: not made with human hands, not subject to decay, lasting forever. The verse thus opens the passage on the believer’s hope beyond death: the very dissolution of the body, far from being final loss, gives way to a better and abiding dwelling. It is the secret of Paul’s endurance — he looks beyond the perishing tent to the eternal house, so that death itself becomes not destruction but exchange. This assurance frames the groaning, the longing, and the confidence that follow.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his courage under affliction in the certainty of the eternal house. The present body is a tent — flimsy, temporary, soon struck down; but awaiting the believer is a building of God, eternal in the heavens. The confident “we know” is not a wish but assured knowledge. The glorified body is set against the dissolving tent: not made with hands, not subject to decay. The very dissolution of the body, far from being final loss, gives way to a better dwelling — so that death becomes not destruction but exchange. This frames the groaning and confidence that follow.
2:
On 2 Corinthians 5:2 — “For in this we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed upon with our house which is from heaven” — the witnesses mark the believer’s groaning and longing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “to be clothed upon” (ependysasthai), to put on over oneself, like an upper garment. Barnes: in the present body we groan (Romans 8:23), earnestly desiring the heavenly state. Ellicott connects the groaning with the “pressure of disease.” Wesley: the rising body “clothed… with what is… incorruptible and immortal.”

The verse gives the inward longing. In this tent we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling. The believer yearns not merely to escape the body but to be invested with the glorious house from heaven.

So the witnesses see Paul voice the believer’s groaning desire for the heavenly state. The witnesses note the word for the longing — “to be clothed upon,” to have the heavenly house put on over the present one, as a garment drawn over the body. They mark that the groaning is twofold: it springs from the burdens, pains, and infirmities of the present body, and it reaches forward in earnest desire for the glory to come. They observe that Paul does not merely wish to be rid of the body (to be “unclothed”) but to be further clothed — the mortal swallowed up of life, the perishable invested with the immortal. The verse thus deepens the hope of verse 1: the believer, weighed down in the tent, sighs for the heavenly dwelling, not out of mere weariness but out of longing for a better state. It expresses the proper Christian attitude toward death and glory — not a Stoic indifference, nor a morbid wish to escape, but an earnest, hopeful yearning to be clothed with the immortal body and to be at home with the Lord, which Paul will unfold in the verses following.

So the witnesses see Paul voice the believer’s groaning desire for the heavenly state. The word for the longing — “to be clothed upon” — pictures the heavenly house put on over the present one, as a garment drawn over the body. The groaning is twofold: from the burdens of the present body, and reaching forward in earnest desire for glory. Paul does not merely wish to be rid of the body but to be further clothed, the mortal swallowed up of life. It expresses the proper Christian attitude — not Stoic indifference nor a morbid wish to escape, but a hopeful yearning to be clothed with immortality and at home with the Lord.
3:
On 2 Corinthians 5:3 — “If so be that being clothed we shall not be found naked” — the witnesses mark a much-interpreted clause.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the difficulty. Robertson: “naked” (gymnoi), disembodied spirits, “without form… and void of all power of activity” (Plummer). Barnes records that the passage “has been interpreted in a great many different ways.” Ellicott: “if, as I believe…” — a confident expectation. The Reformed Calvin restricts the hope to believers, the wicked being stripped to “a disgraceful nakedness.”

The verse adds a condition or assurance. Being clothed with the heavenly house, we shall not be found naked — disembodied. The believer’s hope is not a bare, unclothed state but a glorious re-investing.

So the witnesses approach this admittedly difficult clause with care. The witnesses note Barnes’s candid acknowledgment that it has been read many ways, and the common sense the expositors give: “naked” means the disembodied state of the spirit apart from the body, stripped and without its proper covering. They mark the point Paul presses: the believer’s hope is not to remain a bare, unclothed spirit, but to be “clothed” with the heavenly, glorious body, so as not to be “found naked.” They observe Calvin’s restriction of this blessed clothing to believers — the wicked too are stripped of the body, but appear before God in shameful nakedness, having no glorious investiture. They note Ellicott’s reading of the particles as expressing confident expectation: “if, as I surely believe.” The verse thus, however precisely construed, serves the larger hope: the Christian looks beyond death not to a permanent, naked, disembodied existence but to being clothed upon with the immortal house from heaven. The longing of verse 2 is for a positive glory, not a mere escape — the assurance that those in Christ shall not be left uncovered, but gloriously re-clad.

So the witnesses approach this admittedly difficult clause with care. They note Barnes’s candid acknowledgment that it has been read many ways, and the common sense given: “naked” means the disembodied state of the spirit, stripped of its proper covering. The point Paul presses is that the believer’s hope is not to remain a bare, unclothed spirit, but to be clothed with the glorious body, so as not to be “found naked.” Calvin restricts this blessed clothing to believers — the wicked too are stripped, but appear before God in shameful nakedness. The Christian looks beyond death not to a permanent disembodied existence but to being gloriously re-clad.
4:
On 2 Corinthians 5:4 — “For we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened: not for that we would be unclothed, but clothed upon, that mortality might be swallowed up of life” — the witnesses mark the burdened groaning clarified.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the precise wish. Robertson: “we do not wish to put off the clothing, but to put it on” (ependysasthai) — not a mere disembodied spirit. Ellicott parallels Wisdom 9:15 — the corruptible body presses down the soul. Wesley: “not that we desire to remain without a body… faith does not understand that philosophy.”

The verse clarifies the longing. We groan, burdened, in this tent — not wishing to be unclothed (a bare spirit), but clothed over, that what is mortal may be swallowed up by life. The desire is for glory, not for dissolution.

So the witnesses see Paul refine the meaning of the believer’s groaning. The witnesses note that the groaning is real — the body is a burden, weighed down by afflictions, infirmities, and temptations — yet Paul corrects any misunderstanding: the longing is not simply to be “unclothed,” to escape the body and exist as a disembodied spirit, but to be “clothed upon,” to have the mortal invested with the immortal. They mark the glorious aim: “that mortality might be swallowed up of life” — not the soul stripped bare, but the whole man, body and all, transformed and absorbed into the life of glory. They observe Wesley’s point that faith does not embrace the philosophy that despises the body and seeks mere disembodiment; the Christian hope is bodily resurrection and glorification. The verse thus guards the Christian longing from a false spiritualism: the believer groans not to be rid of the body but to have it transfigured, the perishable swallowed up by life. It is a hope of fulness, not of subtraction — the mortal made immortal, life triumphant over death.

So the witnesses see Paul refine the meaning of the believer’s groaning. The groaning is real — the body a burden, weighed down by afflictions and infirmities — yet Paul corrects any misunderstanding: the longing is not simply to be “unclothed,” to exist as a disembodied spirit, but to be “clothed upon,” the mortal invested with the immortal. The glorious aim is “that mortality might be swallowed up of life” — the whole man transformed into the life of glory. Faith does not embrace the philosophy that despises the body; the Christian hope is bodily resurrection — a hope of fulness, not of subtraction.
5:
On 2 Corinthians 5:5 — “Now he that hath wrought us for the selfsame thing is God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit” — the witnesses mark God’s working and the Spirit’s pledge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the divine author. Barnes: God “hath formed… or made us for this” very thing — the heavenly dwelling. Ellicott: the whole work of God — redemption, new birth, gifts — aims at this consummation. Wesley: “none but God… could have wrought this in us.” The Reformed Calvin: this disposition is “supernatural,” not from mere natural feeling.

The verse traces the longing to God and seals it by the Spirit. It is God who has fashioned us for this very thing — the heavenly glory — and has given us the Spirit as the pledge of it. The hope rests on God’s own work and guarantee.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the believer’s hope in God’s preparing work and the Spirit’s earnest. The witnesses note that the very longing for immortality, and the fitness for glory, is no natural human aspiration but God’s own working: he has “wrought us” — fashioned, prepared, fitted us — for this heavenly destiny, the consummation in which mortality is swallowed up of life. They mark Calvin’s point that this disposition is supernatural, beyond what nature could produce. And God has not left the hope without security: he has given “the earnest of the Spirit” — the Holy Spirit as a down-payment, a first instalment and pledge guaranteeing the full inheritance to come. They observe that the indwelling Spirit is both the foretaste and the assurance of glory. The verse thus rests the whole hope on God: he who prepared us for glory will surely bring us to it, and his Spirit within is the proof and pledge. The believer’s confidence is not in himself but in the God who fashioned him for immortality and sealed him with the Spirit — the ground of the courage Paul will now express.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the believer’s hope in God’s preparing work and the Spirit’s earnest. The very longing for immortality is no natural aspiration but God’s own working: he has “wrought us” — fashioned, fitted us — for this heavenly destiny; the disposition is supernatural (Calvin). And God has not left the hope without security: he has given “the earnest of the Spirit” — the Holy Spirit as a down-payment and pledge guaranteeing the full inheritance. The believer’s confidence is not in himself but in the God who fashioned him for immortality and sealed him with the Spirit.
6:
On 2 Corinthians 5:6 — “Therefore we are always confident, knowing that, whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord” — the witnesses mark the believer’s confidence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the courage. Robertson: “at home in the body” (endemountes), as opposed to away from home. Barnes: “to be of good cheer… full of hope” — Paul not dejected but cheerful. The Reformed Calvin: this courage “has no place in us, unless… maintained by the Spirit of God.”

The verse states the resulting confidence. We are always of good courage, knowing that while we are at home in the body we are absent from the Lord. The believer is cheerful, yet aware that present bodily life means distance from Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul draw confidence from the hope and the Spirit’s earnest. The witnesses note the word — “always confident,” of good courage, full of hope — the very opposite of dejection; Paul faces affliction and death cheerfully. They mark the ground of a certain holy tension he now introduces: to be “at home in the body” is to be “absent from the Lord” — the present life in the mortal body, however blessed, keeps the believer at a distance from the immediate presence of Christ. They observe Calvin’s caution that this courage is not natural bravado but the gift of the Spirit, who alone keeps the soul steady and undismayed. The verse thus turns the hope of glory into present confidence: because the eternal house is sure and the Spirit is the pledge, the believer can be of good courage even now, even in the face of death. Yet the confidence carries a longing — for while embodied here, he is away from his Lord. This sets up the great preference Paul will state: to be absent from the body and present with the Lord.

So the witnesses see Paul draw confidence from the hope and the Spirit’s earnest. The word is “always confident,” of good courage, full of hope — the very opposite of dejection; Paul faces affliction and death cheerfully. Yet he introduces a holy tension: to be “at home in the body” is to be “absent from the Lord” — the present life keeps the believer at a distance from Christ’s immediate presence. Calvin cautions that this courage is not natural bravado but the gift of the Spirit. The hope of glory becomes present confidence, yet carries a longing — for while embodied here, he is away from his Lord.
7:
On 2 Corinthians 5:7 — “For we walk by faith, not by sight” — the witnesses mark the rule of the present life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “by sight” (dia eidous), rather “by appearance.” Ellicott: the word “never means the faculty of seeing, but always the form and fashion of the thing” — we walk not by what we see. Barnes: “to walk” means to live, to conduct oneself; the Christian travels to another country. Wesley: faith is “an evidence… that necessarily implies a kind of seeing him who is invisible.”

The verse states the principle of the believer’s life. We walk by faith, not by sight — ordering our whole life by what God has revealed and promised, not by what is outwardly seen. Faith, not appearance, is our guide.

So the witnesses see Paul explain why the believer can be confident though absent from the Lord. The witnesses note that “walk” means the whole conduct of life — the Christian as a pilgrim journeying to another country. They mark the contrast: “by faith, not by sight” — or, as the expositors clarify, not by “appearance,” not by the outward form and fashion of things seen, but by trust in the unseen realities God has promised. They observe that faith is itself a kind of vision — an evidence of things not seen, a seeing of him who is invisible — yet far beneath the open sight of glory to come. The believer does not yet behold the Lord face to face, nor the eternal house, but lives now by confident trust in them. The verse thus accounts for the present absence from the Lord and the courage that endures it: faith bridges the distance, ordering life by the promises rather than by the seen. It is the governing principle of the Christian walk while embodied here — a life shaped by the unseen and eternal, not by the visible and passing.

So the witnesses see Paul explain why the believer can be confident though absent from the Lord. “Walk” means the whole conduct of life — the Christian as a pilgrim journeying to another country. The contrast is “by faith, not by sight” — or, as the expositors clarify, not by “appearance,” the outward form of things seen, but by trust in the unseen realities God has promised. Faith is itself a kind of vision — a seeing of him who is invisible — yet far beneath the open sight to come. The believer lives now by confident trust; faith bridges the distance, ordering life by the promises rather than by the seen.
8:
On 2 Corinthians 5:8 — “We are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body, and to be present with the Lord” — the witnesses mark the believer’s preference.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the longing. Robertson: “we are well-pleased, we prefer” (eudokoumen); “to be at home with the Lord.” Barnes: “we would prefer to die” (Philippians 1:23). Wesley: this “demonstrates that the happiness of the saints is not deferred till the resurrection.” Ellicott reconciles it with verse 4: he prefers death because it brings him to the Lord.

The verse states the believer’s settled preference. We are confident, and would rather be absent from the body and present with the Lord. To die is to depart from the body into the immediate presence of Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul declare his preference for the presence of the Lord, even at the cost of leaving the body. The witnesses note the resumed confidence and the “willing rather” — he is well-pleased, he prefers, to be absent from the body if it means being at home with the Lord. They mark the precious doctrine the expositors draw: the believer’s blessedness is not wholly deferred to the resurrection, for at death the soul departs to be “present with the Lord” — an immediate, conscious nearness to Christ, as Paul elsewhere says, “to depart and to be with Christ, which is far better.” They observe Ellicott’s reconciling of this with verse 4: Paul does not crave mere disembodiment for its own sake, but accepts even the temporary “unclothed” state gladly because it brings him into the Lord’s presence, awaiting the full clothing at the resurrection. The verse thus crowns the passage on the believer’s hope: death holds no terror, for it ushers the soul into the immediate company of Christ. The preference is not for death itself but for the Lord — and this longing fuels the aim to please him that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul declare his preference for the presence of the Lord, even at the cost of leaving the body. The “willing rather” means he is well-pleased, he prefers, to be absent from the body if it means being at home with the Lord. The precious doctrine the expositors draw is that the believer’s blessedness is not wholly deferred to the resurrection, for at death the soul departs to be “present with the Lord.” Ellicott reconciles this with verse 4: Paul does not crave mere disembodiment, but accepts even the temporary state gladly because it brings him to the Lord. Death holds no terror, ushering the soul into the immediate company of Christ.
9:
On 2 Corinthians 5:9 — “Wherefore we labour, that, whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him” — the witnesses mark the great aim.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ambition. Robertson: “we make it our aim” (philotimoumetha), to be ambitious in the good sense, fond of honour. Adam Clarke: “ambitious to do… every thing consistently with our high vocation.” Ellicott: “our ambition is that… we may be acceptable to him.” The Puritan Henry: “well-grounded hopes of heaven will not encourage sloth.”

The verse states the resulting purpose. Therefore we make it our ambition, whether at home in the body or absent from it, to be acceptable to him. The hope of glory fuels a holy aim to please the Lord.

So the witnesses see Paul turn the hope of glory into a present, governing ambition. The witnesses note the strong word — not mere “labour” but a holy ambition, a striving for honour, the one worthy ambition of the Christian: to be “accepted” or acceptable to the Lord. They mark that this aim holds “whether present or absent” — whether living in the body or departed to be with Christ, in every state the one purpose is to please him. They observe Henry’s point that the believer’s sure hope of heaven does not breed sloth or carelessness but, on the contrary, spurs diligence and self-denial; the assurance of glory makes the Christian more, not less, earnest to please his Lord. The verse thus draws the practical fruit from the doctrine of hope: because we look for the eternal house and long for the Lord’s presence, we make it our consuming aim to be acceptable to him now. It is the hinge between the hope of glory and the motive for holy living — and it leads directly to the solemn reason Paul gives, the judgment seat of Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul turn the hope of glory into a present, governing ambition. The strong word is not mere “labour” but a holy ambition, a striving for honour — the one worthy ambition of the Christian: to be “accepted” or acceptable to the Lord. This aim holds “whether present or absent” — in every state the one purpose is to please him. Henry presses that the believer’s sure hope of heaven does not breed sloth but spurs diligence; the assurance of glory makes the Christian more, not less, earnest to please his Lord. It is the hinge between the hope of glory and the motive for holy living.
10:
On 2 Corinthians 5:10 — “For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things done in his body… whether it be good or bad” — the witnesses mark the universal reckoning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the tribunal. Robertson: “the judgment-seat” (bematos) of Christ, Saviour, Lord, and Judge of all. Ellicott: “must all be made manifest” — the same word as 1 Corinthians 4:5. Wesley: “openly, without covering, where all hidden things will be revealed.” The Reformed Calvin: Paul “constantly sisted himself before the bar of Christ.”

The verse gives the solemn reason for the holy aim. We must all appear before Christ’s judgment seat, each to receive according to what he has done in the body, good or bad. The certainty of judgment grounds the diligence to please him.

So the witnesses see Paul press the universal judgment as the spur to holy living. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “we must all” — apostles and all men alike — appear, or rather be “made manifest,” laid open before the judgment seat of Christ, where every hidden thing is revealed. They mark that Christ, the Saviour and Lord, is also the Judge, and that each will “receive” according to the things done in the body, whether good or bad — a real recompense answering to the life lived. They observe Calvin’s example that Paul kept himself continually before this bar, ordering his life by the awareness of coming judgment. They note that this does not overthrow justification by grace, for the believer’s sins are pardoned in Christ, yet the life and works are brought into account, the genuineness of faith made manifest. The verse thus furnishes the weighty motive for the ambition of verse 9: because all must stand before Christ’s tribunal and receive according to their deeds, the believer makes it his aim to be acceptable to him — living now in view of that day.

So the witnesses see Paul press the universal judgment as the spur to holy living. “We must all” — apostles and all men alike — be made manifest, laid open before the judgment seat of Christ, where every hidden thing is revealed. Christ, the Saviour and Lord, is also the Judge, and each will “receive” according to the things done in the body, good or bad. This does not overthrow justification by grace, for the believer’s sins are pardoned in Christ; yet the life and works are brought into account, the genuineness of faith made manifest. Paul kept himself continually before this bar (Calvin), ordering his life by the awareness of judgment.
11:
On 2 Corinthians 5:11 — “Knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men; but we are made manifest unto God; and I trust also are made manifest in your consciences” — the witnesses mark the motive of persuasion.

The Eastern fathers note the zeal. Chrysostom: knowing “that terrible seat of judgment, we do every thing” to give no offence. The grammatical-historical commentators clarify the phrase. Robertson: “the fear of the Lord” (phobon); “we persuade” (peithomen), conative, we try to persuade. Ellicott and Clarke: “terror” is too strong — rather “the fear of the Lord,” reverential awe, not slavish dread.

The verse gives the spring of Paul’s ministry. Knowing the fear of the Lord, we seek to persuade men; and our integrity is open to God, and, he trusts, to the Corinthians’ consciences. The coming judgment moves him to win souls.

So the witnesses see Paul make the fear of the Lord and the judgment to come the motive of his evangelism. The witnesses note the careful rendering the expositors prefer: not slavish “terror” but the reverential “fear of the Lord” — the awe of him who is Judge of all, born of the foregoing verse. They mark that this fear does not paralyse but propels: “we persuade men” — Paul labours to win them, to bring them to be reconciled and ready for that day. They observe his appeal to transparency: his conduct is “made manifest unto God,” fully open to the divine scrutiny, and he trusts it is also manifest to the Corinthians’ own consciences — an answer to those who maligned him. They note Chrysostom’s observation of the watchful strictness this awareness produced. The verse thus joins the judgment seat to the work of persuasion: because Paul knows the fear of the Lord, he spends himself to persuade men; and because his life is open before God, he can appeal to the Corinthians’ consciences for his integrity — preparing his defence against detractors in the verses following.

So the witnesses see Paul make the fear of the Lord and the judgment to come the motive of his evangelism. The expositors prefer not slavish “terror” but the reverential “fear of the Lord” — the awe of him who is Judge of all. This fear does not paralyse but propels: “we persuade men,” labouring to win them for that day. He appeals to his transparency: his conduct is “made manifest unto God,” fully open to divine scrutiny, and he trusts also to the Corinthians’ consciences — an answer to those who maligned him. Because Paul knows the fear of the Lord, he spends himself to persuade men.
12:
On 2 Corinthians 5:12 — “For we commend not ourselves again unto you, but give you occasion to glory on our behalf, that ye may have somewhat to answer them which glory in appearance, and not in heart” — the witnesses mark the purpose of his self-defence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the aim. Robertson: “occasion of glorying” (aphormen), a base of operations, material to glory; “that ye may have wherewith to answer.” Adam Clarke: not to bespeak their praise but to give them an answer to those who “glory in appearance.” The Puritan Henry: it was not to commend himself but for their sakes.

The verse explains why Paul speaks of himself. Not to commend himself again, but to give the Corinthians ground to glory on his behalf, so they may answer those who glory in outward appearance, not in heart. His defence is for their sake.

So the witnesses see Paul guard his self-vindication from the charge of mere self-praise. The witnesses note his disclaimer: he is not commending himself afresh, as if seeking their good opinion for its own sake; rather, he is furnishing them with material — a “base of operations” — for glorying on his behalf. They mark the practical purpose: that the Corinthians might have a ready answer to the false teachers, those who “glory in appearance, and not in heart” — men who boasted in outward credentials, eloquence, and show, while lacking inward sincerity. They observe Henry’s point that Paul’s speaking of himself was not for his own sake but for theirs and the gospel’s. The verse thus reveals the motive behind his frequent self-references: not vanity, but the defence of his ministry against detractors, for the Corinthians’ benefit. He arms them to vindicate their true apostle against those who prized show over substance — and prepares the great statement of what truly moved him: not appearance, but the constraining love of Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul guard his self-vindication from the charge of mere self-praise. He is not commending himself afresh, as if seeking their good opinion for its own sake; rather, he is furnishing them with material — a “base of operations” — for glorying on his behalf. The practical purpose is that the Corinthians might have a ready answer to the false teachers, those who “glory in appearance, and not in heart” — men boasting in outward credentials while lacking inward sincerity. His speaking of himself was not for his own sake but for theirs and the gospel’s, arming them to vindicate their true apostle.
13:
On 2 Corinthians 5:13 — “For whether we be beside ourselves, it is to God: or whether we be sober, it is for your cause” — the witnesses mark Paul’s answer to a sneer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the charge. Robertson: “beside ourselves” (exestemen), to stand out of oneself, ecstasy — literary plural, of Paul himself. Adam Clarke recalls Festus’s “Paul, thou art beside thyself.” Ellicott: detractors sneered at his visions and ecstasies. Wesley: “transported beyond ourselves… it is to God.”

The verse answers a taunt about his zeal. If we are beside ourselves, it is to God; if we are sober, it is for your sake. Whether in ecstatic fervour or in calm reason, Paul acts for God and for the Corinthians.

So the witnesses see Paul turn a sneer into a confession of pure motive. The witnesses note the charge behind the verse: detractors had mocked Paul as “beside himself” — carried away, fanatical, perhaps deranged, as Festus had said, or given to visions and ecstasies they despised. Paul does not deny his fervour but reframes it: whatever transports him beyond ordinary bounds is “to God” — an ecstasy of devotion offered to him, understood by God if not by men; and his sober, measured ministry is “for your cause” — the calm, plain dealing he uses for the Corinthians’ benefit. They mark that both his heights of zeal and his composed teaching spring from the same selfless source, directed wholly to God and to their good, never to his own reputation. The verse thus deflects the taunt by laying bare his motive: nothing for self, all for God and for them. It prepares the great declaration of what governs him in every state — the love of Christ that constrains, leaving no room for self-seeking, whether in rapture or in reason.

So the witnesses see Paul turn a sneer into a confession of pure motive. Detractors had mocked him as “beside himself” — carried away, fanatical, given to visions and ecstasies they despised. Paul does not deny his fervour but reframes it: whatever transports him beyond ordinary bounds is “to God” — an ecstasy of devotion God understands; and his sober, measured ministry is “for your cause” — the plain dealing he uses for the Corinthians’ benefit. Both his heights of zeal and his composed teaching spring from the same selfless source, directed wholly to God and to their good, never to his own reputation.
14:
On 2 Corinthians 5:14 — “For the love of Christ constraineth us; because we thus judge, that if one died for all, then were all dead” — the witnesses mark the constraining love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the genitive and the verb. Robertson: “the love of Christ” (subjective), Christ’s love for Paul; “constraineth” (synechei), holds together, presses on every side. Ellicott: Paul’s uniform usage is “decisive in favour” of Christ’s love for us (Romans 5:8). Clarke and Wesley include also “our love to him.”

The verse names the master-motive of Paul’s life. The love of Christ constrains us, since we judge that if one died for all, then all died. Christ’s dying love, embracing all, controls and impels the apostle.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the spring of all his zeal: the constraining love of Christ. The witnesses note the force of “constraineth” — it holds him together, hems him in, presses on every side, and bears him irresistibly along, as winds and tides waft a ship to harbour. They mark the debate over “the love of Christ”: most, with Ellicott and Robertson, take it as Christ’s love for us — his dying love, shown in verse 15 — while Clarke and Wesley would include also the believer’s answering love to Christ; the two are intertwined, but the ground is Christ’s love first. They observe the reasoning that fuels it: “if one died for all, then were all dead” — Christ’s death for all proves that all were dead in sin, needing his dying; and his representative death involves them. The verse thus lays bare the deepest motive of Paul’s ministry: not ambition, not fear of men, but the overwhelming love of Christ who died for all. That love, once apprehended, leaves no room for self — it constrains the believer to live wholly for him, as the next verse declares.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the spring of all his zeal: the constraining love of Christ. The force of “constraineth” is that it holds him together, hems him in, presses on every side, and bears him irresistibly along, as winds and tides waft a ship to harbour. They mark the debate over “the love of Christ”: most take it as Christ’s love for us — his dying love — while Clarke and Wesley include also the believer’s answering love; the ground is Christ’s love first. The reasoning fuels it: “if one died for all, then were all dead.” That love, once apprehended, leaves no room for self.
15:
On 2 Corinthians 5:15 — “And that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them, and rose again” — the witnesses mark the purpose of Christ’s death.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the obligation. Robertson: the high doctrine of the atoning death carries “a correspondingly high obligation” — selfishness ruled out. Adam Clarke: bought by his blood, “they are not their own.” Wesley: live “not… unto themselves… but unto him,” from the moment they know him.

The verse states the end of Christ’s dying. He died for all, that those who live should no longer live for themselves but for him who died and rose for them. The atonement claims the whole life of the redeemed.

So the witnesses see Paul draw the great obligation from Christ’s death: a life no longer self-centred but Christ-centred. The witnesses note the purpose clause: Christ died for all precisely “that they which live” — those made alive by his death — “should not henceforth live unto themselves.” They mark the force of the logic: if Christ died for them, they are bought with his blood, no longer their own; selfishness is ruled out, and the whole of life is owed to him. They observe that the positive aim is to live “unto him which died for them, and rose again” — the risen Lord, to whom the redeemed now belong wholly, in all righteousness and holiness. They note that this is the very constraint of verse 14 worked out: Christ’s love, in dying for all, lays on all who live by him the debt of a wholly devoted life. The verse thus turns the doctrine of the atonement into the rule of Christian living: those for whom Christ died are not their own but his, and must live not for self but for the One who died and rose for them — the death of self-interest, the dawn of a new creation, which Paul will now describe.

So the witnesses see Paul draw the great obligation from Christ’s death: a life no longer self-centred but Christ-centred. Christ died for all precisely “that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves.” If Christ died for them, they are bought with his blood, no longer their own; selfishness is ruled out. The positive aim is to live “unto him which died for them, and rose again” — the risen Lord, to whom the redeemed wholly belong. This is the constraint of verse 14 worked out: those for whom Christ died are not their own but his, and must live not for self but for the One who died and rose for them.
16:
On 2 Corinthians 5:16 — “Wherefore henceforth know we no man after the flesh: yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we him no more” — the witnesses mark the new standard of judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the changed view. Robertson: “after the flesh” (kata sarka), the fleshy way of looking at men — “worldly standards and distinctions.” The Puritan Henry sees here weanedness from the world. Calvin: “we do not judge according to external appearance.” Wesley: not by “former state, country, descent, nobility, riches… wisdom.”

The verse marks the new way of regarding others. From now on we know no one after the flesh; even Christ we no longer know in that way. The new life in Christ gives new standards, above worldly distinctions.

So the witnesses see Paul describe the transformed judgment that flows from living unto Christ. The witnesses note that to “know a man after the flesh” is to value him by outward, worldly standards — descent, nation, rank, wealth, eloquence, the very things the false teachers prized. Having died to self and risen to live for Christ, Paul no longer esteems men so; he sees past the external to the new creation. They mark the striking application even to Christ: “though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now… no more” — whatever merely earthly or national conception of the Messiah Paul once held (the Jewish hope of a temporal deliverer), he has abandoned for the true, spiritual knowledge of the crucified and risen Lord. They observe Henry’s reading of this as weanedness from the world, part of regeneration. The verse thus shows the new vision that the constraining love produces: the believer reckons men, and even Christ, no longer by the flesh but by the Spirit — preparing the great declaration that anyone in Christ is a new creation.

So the witnesses see Paul describe the transformed judgment that flows from living unto Christ. To “know a man after the flesh” is to value him by outward, worldly standards — descent, rank, wealth, eloquence, the very things the false teachers prized. Having died to self, Paul sees past the external to the new creation. The striking application even to Christ — “though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now… no more” — means whatever merely earthly conception of the Messiah Paul once held, he has abandoned for the true knowledge of the crucified and risen Lord. The believer reckons men, and even Christ, no longer by the flesh but by the Spirit.
17:
On 2 Corinthians 5:17 — “Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new” — the witnesses mark the new creation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the words. Robertson: “a new creature” (kaine ktisis), a fresh start, fresh creation; “old things passed away.” Adam Clarke: a genuine Christian, with Christ in his heart, “new creation.” The Puritan Henry: regeneration. Wesley: “only the power that makes a world can make a Christian… the old things… of their own accord, even as snow in spring.”

The verse proclaims the radical change in the believer. If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away, and all has become new. Union with Christ remakes the whole man.

So the witnesses see Paul declare the wonder of regeneration in Christ. The witnesses note the condition — “in Christ,” united to him by faith as the branch in the vine — and the astonishing result: “a new creature,” or new creation. They mark the force of the figure: nothing less than a creative act of God, for, as Wesley says, only the power that makes a world can make a Christian; the change is not mere reform but a new making. They observe the sweep of it: “old things are passed away” — the old life, old loves, old standards, old enmity — gone, as Wesley adds, of their own accord, like snow in spring; “all things are become new” — new heart, new views, new desires, new aims. They note the triumphant “behold,” calling attention to the present, visible, undeniable change. The verse thus crowns the passage on the transformed life: the one who lives unto Christ, and knows men no more after the flesh, is himself a new creation — remade by God. It is the great evangelical statement of the new birth, and the ground of all that follows on reconciliation.

So the witnesses see Paul declare the wonder of regeneration in Christ. The condition is “in Christ,” united to him by faith as the branch in the vine; the result is “a new creature,” or new creation. The figure is nothing less than a creative act of God, for, as Wesley says, only the power that makes a world can make a Christian. “Old things are passed away” — the old life, loves, standards, enmity — gone, like snow in spring; “all things are become new.” The triumphant “behold” calls attention to the present, undeniable change. It is the great evangelical statement of the new birth.
18:
On 2 Corinthians 5:18 — “And all things are of God, who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation” — the witnesses mark the author of reconciliation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the doctrine. Robertson: “reconciled” (katallaxantos), one of Paul’s great words, to reconcile, change from enmity. Ellicott: “all things” here are the new things of the new creation. The Reformed Calvin: he speaks of “the grace of regeneration… conferred peculiarly upon his elect.”

The verse traces the new creation to God and names the ministry. All this is of God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation. God is the author both of the reconciling and of its proclamation.

So the witnesses see Paul ascribe the whole work of the new creation to God and introduce the ministry of reconciliation. The witnesses note the emphatic “all things are of God” — the new birth, the new standards, the new life, all flowing from his grace, not from man. They mark the heart of the gospel here stated: God “hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ” — removing the enmity, restoring the broken relationship between sinful man and a holy God, and that through the mediation of Christ. They observe that reconciliation is God’s own act, originating in his grace; he is not the reconciled but the Reconciler, taking the initiative to bring rebels back. They note the further gift: “the ministry of reconciliation” — God has entrusted to Paul and his fellow-labourers the task of proclaiming this reconciliation to men. The verse thus moves from the new creation in the individual to its divine source and its appointed means: God reconciles through Christ, and sends ministers to publish it. It opens the great passage on the message of reconciliation, the embassy, and the appeal Paul will make.

So the witnesses see Paul ascribe the whole work of the new creation to God and introduce the ministry of reconciliation. The emphatic “all things are of God” traces the new birth and new life to his grace, not to man. The heart of the gospel is here: God “hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ” — removing the enmity, restoring the broken relationship, through the mediation of Christ. Reconciliation is God’s own act; he is not the reconciled but the Reconciler, taking the initiative to bring rebels back. And he gave “the ministry of reconciliation,” entrusting the proclamation to his ministers.
19:
On 2 Corinthians 5:19 — “To wit, that God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them; and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation” — the witnesses mark the heart of the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the compact statement. Robertson: “to wit, that” (hos hoti), “how that”; “not reckoning” (me logizomenos) their trespasses. Barnes: “one of those emphatic passages in which Paul compresses… the whole plan.” Adam Clarke expounds “God was in Christ.” Wesley: “the whole Godhead, but more eminently God the Father.”

The verse states the gospel in brief. God was in Christ, reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them, and has committed to us the word of reconciliation. The whole plan of salvation is compressed here.

So the witnesses see Paul compress the entire gospel into a single sentence. The witnesses note the three great truths: first, “God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself” — the reconciling work is God’s, accomplished in and through Christ, embracing the world of sinners; second, “not imputing their trespasses unto them” — the manner of reconciliation, by the non-reckoning of sin, the forgiveness grounded (as verse 21 shows) on Christ’s bearing it; and third, “hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation” — the message entrusted to the ministers. They mark Barnes’s observation that this is one of those emphatic verses compressing the whole plan of salvation. They observe that the reconciliation is God’s initiative, God’s achievement in Christ, offered freely to the world by the not-imputing of sin. The verse thus unfolds the ministry of reconciliation of verse 18: its substance is that God in Christ has reconciled the world by forgiving sin, and its proclamation is the “word of reconciliation” placed in the apostles’ hands — the ground of the ambassadorial appeal that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul compress the entire gospel into a single sentence. Three great truths: “God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself” — the reconciling work God’s, in and through Christ, embracing the world of sinners; “not imputing their trespasses unto them” — the manner, by the non-reckoning of sin, grounded (as v21 shows) on Christ’s bearing it; and “hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation” — the message entrusted to the ministers. Barnes notes this is one of those emphatic verses compressing the whole plan of salvation. The reconciliation is God’s initiative, offered freely by the not-imputing of sin.
20:
On 2 Corinthians 5:20 — “Now then we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God did beseech you by us: we pray you in Christ’s stead, be ye reconciled to God” — the witnesses mark the embassy and the appeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the dignity of the office. Robertson: “ambassadors” (presbeuomen), the proper term for the Emperor’s Legate; on Christ’s behalf and in his stead (Ephesians 6:20). The Reformed Calvin: this gives “authority to the embassy.” Wesley marks the “unparalleled condescension and divinely tender mercy” of God beseeching.

The verse names the ministers’ office and voices the gospel plea. We are ambassadors for Christ; God himself beseeches through us; in Christ’s stead we pray, be reconciled to God. The message of reconciliation becomes an entreaty.

So the witnesses see Paul define the minister’s office and utter the gospel’s great appeal. The witnesses note the dignity of “ambassador” — the official representative of a sovereign, sent to treat with another power; the preacher stands “for Christ,” on his behalf and in his place, bearing his full authority. They mark the astonishing condescension: “as though God did beseech you by us” — the offended God, against whom man has rebelled, himself entreating the rebels to be reconciled; Wesley wonders at the “divinely tender mercy” of it. They observe the heart of the plea: “be ye reconciled to God” — not that God must be reconciled to man (he has already reconciled the world in Christ), but that man must lay down his enmity and accept the reconciliation freely offered. They note Calvin’s point that the ambassadorial office lends divine authority to the message. The verse thus presents the gospel as God’s own earnest entreaty, conveyed through his ambassadors: the Reconciler stoops to beseech the rebel to be reconciled — and grounds the appeal in the great exchange of the next verse.

So the witnesses see Paul define the minister’s office and utter the gospel’s great appeal. “Ambassador” is the official representative of a sovereign, sent to treat with another power; the preacher stands “for Christ,” on his behalf and in his place, bearing his authority. The astonishing condescension is “as though God did beseech you by us” — the offended God himself entreating the rebels. The heart of the plea is “be ye reconciled to God” — not that God must be reconciled to man, but that man must lay down his enmity and accept the reconciliation freely offered. The Reconciler stoops to beseech the rebel.
21:
On 2 Corinthians 5:21 — “For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him” — the witnesses mark the great exchange.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the precise terms. Robertson: “him who knew no sin” (ton me gnonta hamartian), a definite claim of Christ’s sinlessness (1 Peter 2:22; Hebrews 4:15). Adam Clarke reads “sin” as a “sin-offering.” Wesley: “a sin offering… that we might be made the righteousness of God” — we who knew no righteousness. The Reformed Calvin: no return to favour except “founded on the sacrifice of Christ alone.”

The verse states the ground of reconciliation in the great exchange. God made the sinless Christ to be sin for us, that in him we might become the righteousness of God. Our sin laid on him, his righteousness given to us.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the whole gospel of reconciliation in the wondrous exchange of the cross. The witnesses note first the sinlessness of Christ — “who knew no sin” — a definite claim that the Saviour was without sin, qualified to be the substitute. They mark the staggering statement: God “made him to be sin for us” — some, with Clarke and Wesley, reading “sin” as a sin-offering, the sacrifice for sin; others as the imputation of our guilt to the sinless One, treated as sin in our place; the witnesses see both the sacrificial and the substitutionary force, Christ bearing our sin that it might be punished in him. They observe the glorious end: “that we might be made the righteousness of God in him” — we who had no righteousness receive, in union with Christ, a righteousness not our own but God’s, reckoned to us. They note Calvin’s insistence that there is no return to God’s favour except founded on this sacrifice alone. The verse thus crowns the ministry of reconciliation with its deepest ground: the sinless Christ made sin, the guilty made righteous — the great exchange by which God reconciles the world, and the heart of the gospel the ambassador proclaims.
